title: Reproduction and Variation
subtitle: Human and plant reproduction, puberty, variation, inheritance and classification.

studyGuide.heading: Human reproductive systems
studyGuide.html:
• Male: testes make sperm; the penis delivers sperm; sperm travel along the sperm duct.
  • Female: ovaries store and release egg cells (ova); the oviduct (fallopian tube) carries the egg and is where fertilisation happens; the uterus (womb) is where a baby develops.

studyGuide.heading: Fertilisation and pregnancy
studyGuide.html:
• Fertilisation = the joining of a sperm nucleus and an egg nucleus, forming a single cell called a zygote.
  • The zygote divides to form an embryo, which settles into the uterus lining (implantation).
  • The placenta lets the baby receive food and oxygen and remove waste, through the umbilical cord; the amniotic fluid cushions and protects the baby.
  • Gestation (pregnancy) in humans lasts about 9 months.
studyGuide.mnemonic: Fertilisation = sperm + egg joining. Implantation = the embryo planting into the uterus wall.

studyGuide.heading: Puberty and the menstrual cycle
studyGuide.html:
• Puberty is when the body changes from a child to an adult who can reproduce, controlled by hormones.
  • Changes include growth spurts, body hair, voice deepening (males), breast development and periods starting (females).
  • The menstrual cycle is about 28 days: the uterus lining builds up ready for a fertilised egg; if none implants, the lining breaks down (a period).

studyGuide.heading: Plant reproduction
studyGuide.html:
Flowers are the reproductive organs of many plants.

  • Stamen (male) = anther (makes pollen) + filament.
  • Carpel (female) = stigma + style + ovary (contains ovules).
  • Pollination = transfer of pollen from anther to stigma (by insects or wind).
  • Fertilisation follows when a pollen nucleus joins an ovule; the ovule becomes a seed and the ovary becomes a fruit.

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.s{stroke:#1f2a44;stroke-width:1.5;fill:none}.t{font:9px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}</style><path class='s' d='M150 140 V70'/><ellipse cx='150' cy='62' rx='10' ry='14' fill='#f0a35a'/><line class='s' x1='150' y1='48' x2='150' y2='30'/><circle cx='150' cy='26' r='6' fill='#e0524d'/><text class='t' x='162' y='26'>stigma</text><text class='t' x='162' y='62'>ovary</text><line class='s' x1='110' y1='70' x2='110' y2='40'/><circle cx='110' cy='36' r='7' fill='#f5d020'/><text class='t' x='70' y='36'>anther</text><line class='s' x1='190' y1='70' x2='190' y2='40'/><circle cx='190' cy='36' r='7' fill='#f5d020'/><path class='s' d='M120 90 Q150 110 180 90'/><path class='s' d='M125 100 Q150 125 175 100'/></svg>
diagram.caption: Stamen (anther + filament) is male; carpel (stigma, style, ovary) is female.

studyGuide.heading: Variation
studyGuide.html:
Variation = the differences between individuals.

  • Inherited (genetic) variation — passed from parents in genes (e.g. eye colour, natural hair colour, blood group, earlobe shape).
  • Environmental variation — caused by surroundings/lifestyle (e.g. a scar, language spoken, a suntan).
  • Many features are caused by both (e.g. height, weight).
  • Continuous variation (a range, e.g. height) vs discontinuous variation (distinct groups, e.g. blood group).

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.ax{stroke:#1f2a44;stroke-width:1.5}.c{fill:#5b8def}.d{fill:#2b9d6b}.t{font:bold 10px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}.s{font:8px Nunito;fill:#6b7790}.lab{font:9px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}</style><line class='ax' x1='30' y1='15' x2='30' y2='110'/><line class='ax' x1='30' y1='110' x2='172' y2='110'/><rect class='c' x='36' y='80' width='20' height='30'/><rect class='c' x='57' y='55' width='20' height='55'/><rect class='c' x='78' y='37' width='20' height='73'/><rect class='c' x='99' y='37' width='20' height='73'/><rect class='c' x='120' y='55' width='20' height='55'/><rect class='c' x='141' y='80' width='20' height='30'/><text class='t' x='100' y='128' text-anchor='middle'>Continuous</text><text class='s' x='100' y='140' text-anchor='middle'>smooth range (height)</text><line class='ax' x1='210' y1='15' x2='210' y2='110'/><line class='ax' x1='210' y1='110' x2='350' y2='110'/><rect class='d' x='220' y='65' width='22' height='45'/><rect class='d' x='252' y='42' width='22' height='68'/><rect class='d' x='284' y='78' width='22' height='32'/><rect class='d' x='316' y='86' width='22' height='24'/><text class='lab' x='231' y='121' text-anchor='middle'>A</text><text class='lab' x='263' y='121' text-anchor='middle'>B</text><text class='lab' x='295' y='121' text-anchor='middle'>AB</text><text class='lab' x='327' y='121' text-anchor='middle'>O</text><text class='t' x='284' y='137' text-anchor='middle'>Discontinuous</text><text class='s' x='284' y='148' text-anchor='middle'>groups (blood group)</text></svg>
diagram.caption: Continuous = a smooth range with no gaps (height). Discontinuous = separate groups with nothing in between (blood group).

studyGuide.mnemonic: Inherited = you were born with it (in your genes). Environmental = it happened to you during life.

studyGuide.heading: Inheritance and classification
studyGuide.html:
• Genes carry instructions, are found on chromosomes in the nucleus, and are passed from parents to offspring — this is why offspring resemble their parents.
  • Sexual reproduction mixes genes from two parents → variety. Asexual reproduction uses one parent → identical clones, no variation.
  • Living things are classified into groups (e.g. the five kingdoms; vertebrates: fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds, mammals).
  • A classification key uses yes/no questions about features to identify an organism.

flashcards.front: Where are egg cells (ova) released from?
Where does fertilisation happen in a human?

flashcards.back: The joining of a sperm nucleus and an egg nucleus to form a zygote.
Lets the baby get food and oxygen and remove waste from the mother's blood.
Cushions and protects the developing baby.
Transfer of pollen from the anther to the stigma.
Passed in genes from parents, e.g. natural eye colour, blood group, earlobe shape.
Caused by surroundings/lifestyle, e.g. a scar, a suntan, language spoken.
Continuous = a range (e.g. height); discontinuous = distinct groups (e.g. blood group).
Sexual = two parents, variation; asexual = one parent, identical clones.
A series of questions about features used to identify an organism.

commonMistakes.title: Saying fertilisation happens in the uterus
commonMistakes.detail: Fertilisation happens in the oviduct; the uterus is where the baby develops afterwards.

commonMistakes.title: Mixing up the stamen and carpel
commonMistakes.detail: Stamen (anther + filament) is male; carpel (stigma, style, ovary) is female.

commonMistakes.title: Confusing pollination and fertilisation
commonMistakes.detail: Pollination = pollen moved to the stigma; fertilisation = nuclei joining afterwards.

commonMistakes.title: Calling height purely inherited
commonMistakes.detail: Height is affected by BOTH genes and the environment (e.g. diet).

commonMistakes.title: Saying a suntan is inherited
commonMistakes.detail: A suntan is environmental — caused by the sun, not passed in genes.

commonMistakes.title: Thinking asexual reproduction gives variation
commonMistakes.detail: Asexual reproduction produces genetically identical clones — no variation.

questions.q: Where are sperm cells made?
questions.explanation: Sperm are made in the testes.
questions.hint: The male organ that makes sperm.

questions.q: Where does a baby develop?
questions.explanation: The baby develops in the uterus (womb).
questions.hint: Also called the womb.

questions.q: Fertilisation is the joining of…
options: A sperm nucleus and an egg nucleus
questions.explanation: Fertilisation joins a sperm nucleus with an egg nucleus to form a zygote.
questions.hint: One from each parent.

questions.q: Which flower part makes pollen?
questions.explanation: The anther (part of the stamen) makes pollen.
questions.hint: It's part of the male stamen.

questions.q: Pollination is the transfer of pollen from the anther to the…
questions.explanation: Pollination moves pollen from the anther to the stigma.
questions.hint: Where does pollen need to land?

questions.q: Which is an example of inherited variation?
questions.explanation: Natural eye colour is determined by genes, so it is inherited.
questions.hint: Which one were you born with?

questions.q: State the job of the placenta.
questions.modelAnswer: The placenta allows the developing baby to receive food and oxygen from the mother's blood and to pass waste products back, without the two bloods mixing.
questions.hint: How does the baby get food and oxygen?
questions.examMistake: Saying the mother's and baby's blood mix — they don't.

questions.q: Where in a human female does fertilisation take place?
questions.modelAnswer: Fertilisation takes place in the oviduct (also called the fallopian tube).
questions.hint: Not the uterus — the tube the egg travels along.
questions.examMistake: Saying the uterus — that's where the baby develops later.

questions.q: Roughly how long is a human pregnancy (gestation)?
questions.hint: About three-quarters of a year.

questions.q: Asexual reproduction produces offspring that are…
questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction has one parent and produces identical clones.
questions.hint: One parent, no mixing of genes.

questions.q: Name the female parts of a flower.
questions.modelAnswer: The female part is the carpel, which is made of the stigma, the style and the ovary (which contains the ovules).
questions.hint: Three parts make up the carpel.
questions.examMistake: Including the anther or filament — those are male.

questions.q: Puberty is mainly controlled by…
questions.explanation: Puberty changes are controlled by hormones.
questions.hint: Chemical messengers in the body.

questions.q: Explain the difference between pollination and fertilisation in a plant.
questions.modelAnswer: Pollination is the transfer of pollen from the anther of one flower to the stigma of a flower (by insects or wind). Fertilisation happens afterwards, when the nucleus from the pollen grain travels down to the ovule and joins with the egg nucleus inside the ovary. So pollination is the delivery of pollen and fertilisation is the joining of the nuclei.
questions.hint: One is moving pollen; the other is the nuclei joining.
questions.examMistake: Treating pollination and fertilisation as the same event.

questions.q: Which feature is caused by BOTH genes and environment?
questions.explanation: Height is affected by inherited genes and by diet/environment.
questions.hint: Which one can change with diet but is also inherited?

questions.q: Describe what happens to the egg between release from the ovary and becoming an embryo.
questions.modelAnswer: The egg is released from the ovary and travels into the oviduct. If a sperm meets it there, the sperm nucleus joins the egg nucleus in fertilisation, forming a single cell called a zygote. The zygote then divides repeatedly to form a ball of cells called an embryo, which travels to the uterus and implants in the uterus lining.
questions.hint: Ovary → oviduct → fertilise → zygote → embryo → uterus.
questions.examMistake: Getting the order of zygote and embryo wrong.

questions.q: Why does sexual reproduction produce variation but asexual reproduction does not?
options: Sexual reproduction mixes genes from two parents
questions.explanation: Sexual reproduction combines genes from two parents giving variety; asexual uses one parent so offspring are identical.
questions.hint: How many parents and is there gene mixing?

questions.q: Explain the difference between continuous and discontinuous variation, with an example of each.
questions.modelAnswer: Continuous variation is when a feature can take any value within a range, with no distinct groups, such as height or hand span — shown on a line graph or histogram. Discontinuous variation is when a feature falls into clear, separate categories with nothing in between, such as blood group (A, B, AB, O) or tongue rolling — shown on a bar chart.
questions.hint: One is a smooth range, the other is distinct groups.
questions.examMistake: Giving examples that don't match the type (e.g. blood group as continuous).

questions.q: What is the job of the amniotic fluid?
questions.explanation: Amniotic fluid surrounds and protects the baby from bumps.
questions.hint: Think of it as a protective cushion.

questions.q: Explain why offspring resemble their parents but are not identical to them (in sexual reproduction).
questions.modelAnswer: Offspring inherit genes from both parents, carried on chromosomes in the nucleus, so they show features from each parent and therefore resemble them. However, because they get a mixture of genes — half from each parent in a unique combination — they are not identical to either parent or to their siblings.
questions.hint: Genes come from two parents in a new combination.
questions.examMistake: Forgetting that genes come from BOTH parents.

questions.q: A classification key works by…
options: Asking a series of questions about features to narrow down the identity
questions.explanation: A key uses yes/no questions about observable features to identify an organism.
questions.hint: Step-by-step questions about features.

questions.q: Insect-pollinated flowers are often brightly coloured and scented. Explain why.
questions.modelAnswer: Bright colours and scent attract insects such as bees to the flower. As the insect feeds on nectar, pollen sticks to its body, and it carries the pollen to the next flower's stigma, helping pollination. Wind-pollinated flowers do not need to attract insects, so they are usually dull and unscented.
questions.hint: What are the colour and scent FOR?
questions.examMistake: Not linking the features to attracting pollinating insects.

questions.q: During the menstrual cycle, a period (bleeding) happens when…
options: No fertilised egg implants, so the uterus lining breaks down
questions.explanation: If no fertilised egg implants, the built-up uterus lining is shed as a period.
questions.hint: What happens to the lining if there's no pregnancy?

questions.q: Compare the adaptations of an egg cell and a sperm cell to their functions.
questions.modelAnswer: A sperm cell is small with a tail (flagellum) so it can swim to the egg, and it has many mitochondria to provide energy for swimming; it carries the father's genetic information in its nucleus. An egg cell is much larger and cannot move; it contains a food store (yolk) to nourish the early embryo and the mother's genetic information, and after fertilisation its membrane changes to stop other sperm entering. Both carry a nucleus with genes, but each is specialised for its role.
questions.hint: Sperm = small and mobile; egg = large with food store.
questions.examMistake: Listing features without linking them to fertilisation/nourishing the embryo.

questions.q: Identical twins have exactly the same genes but can still differ slightly. Use this to explain inherited vs environmental variation.
questions.modelAnswer: Identical twins come from one fertilised egg that split, so they have the same genes and therefore identical inherited features such as natural eye colour and blood group. Any differences between them — such as one being more muscular, having a scar, or speaking a different language — must be caused by the environment and lifestyle, not genes. This shows that some variation is inherited (genetic) and some is caused by the environment.
questions.hint: Same genes → differences must come from where?
questions.examMistake: Claiming twin differences are genetic — they can't be if the genes are identical.

questions.q: A gardener wants many identical copies of a prize plant. Which method is best and why?
options: Asexual reproduction (e.g. cuttings), because offspring are genetically identical clones
questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction (cuttings) produces clones, keeping the prize features identical.
questions.hint: Identical copies need no gene mixing.

questions.q: Explain why the variation produced by sexual reproduction is an advantage for a species' survival.
questions.modelAnswer: Sexual reproduction mixes genes from two parents, so offspring vary. If the environment changes — for example a new disease or a colder climate — some individuals will, by chance, have features that help them survive. These survivors reproduce and pass on those genes, so the species as a whole is more likely to survive change. A population of identical clones could all be wiped out by the same threat.
questions.hint: Variety means some individuals can cope when conditions change.
questions.examMistake: Not explaining why variation helps when the environment changes.

questions.q: After fertilisation in a flower, describe what the ovule, ovary and petals become or do.
questions.modelAnswer: After fertilisation, each fertilised ovule develops into a seed, which contains the embryo plant and a food store. The ovary surrounding the ovules grows and becomes the fruit, which protects the seeds and often helps with their dispersal. The petals are no longer needed to attract pollinators, so they wither and fall off.
questions.hint: Ovule → seed; ovary → fruit; petals → ?
questions.examMistake: Swapping what the ovule and ovary become.

questions.q: A student measures the hand spans of 100 pupils and gets a smooth range of values. This is an example of…
options: Discontinuous variation, shown on a bar chart
Continuous variation, shown on a histogram/line graph
questions.explanation: A continuous range of values is continuous variation, displayed as a histogram.
questions.hint: Smooth range = which type, and which graph?

questions.q: Wind-pollinated grasses produce huge amounts of light, smooth pollen and have feathery stigmas hanging outside the flower. Explain how each feature aids pollination.
questions.modelAnswer: Wind pollination is random, so producing huge amounts of light, smooth pollen increases the chance that some is blown far enough and lands on another flower. Feathery stigmas hanging outside the flower have a large surface area exposed to the wind, so they are more likely to catch pollen drifting in the air. The flowers don't need bright petals because they don't rely on insects.
questions.hint: Wind is random — quantity and catching area matter.
questions.examMistake: Saying wind-pollinated flowers attract insects — they don't need to.

questions.q: Using a classification key idea, write two yes/no questions that could separate a fish, a bird and a snake.
questions.modelAnswer: Question 1: 'Does it have feathers?' If yes → it is the bird; if no → go to question 2. Question 2: 'Does it have fins / live in water with gills?' If yes → it is the fish; if no → it is the snake. Each question uses a clear, observable feature to split the organisms into smaller groups until each is identified.
questions.hint: Use one clear feature per question to split the group.
questions.examMistake: Using vague questions like 'is it nice?' instead of observable features.
